title: Sum of Subarray Ranges
description: Compute sum of max minus min for all subarrays

examples.explanation:
Array: [1,2,3], n=3
Subarrays:
1. [1]: max=1, min=1, diff=0, total=0
2. [1,2]: max=2, min=1, diff=1, total=1
3. [1,2,3]: max=3, min=1, diff=2, total=3
4. [2]: max=2, min=2, diff=0, total=3
5. [2,3]: max=3, min=2, diff=1, total=4
6. [3]: max=3, min=3, diff=0, total=4
Final total = 4

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all subarrays by nested loops, compute max and min for each subarray directly, and sum their differences. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Sum of Subarray Ranges
ladders.desc: Complete O(n³) solution computing sum of (max-min) for all subarrays
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the Sum of Subarray Ranges problem. The intuition is straightforward: to find the sum of all subarray ranges, we need to consider every possible contiguous subarray, compute its maximum and minimum values, subtract them, and accumulate these differences. This approach works because it exhaustively examines all possibilities - there are n(n+1)/2 subarrays in an array of length n, and for each subarray we compute max and min by scanning its elements. The algorithm uses three nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index i, the middle loop fixes the ending index j (where j ≥ i), and the inner loop scans from i to j to find max and min. For each subarray [i..j], we initialize max_val and min_val with the first element, then iterate through remaining elements updating these values. The difference (max_val - min_val) is added to a running total. Time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) subarrays and each requires O(n) to compute max/min. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement - it's the most naive but correct implementation. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to reset max/min for each subarray, 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, 3) Not handling empty arrays or single-element arrays correctly. The real pattern is: initialize total = 0; for i from 0 to n-1: for j from i to n-1: initialize max_val = arr[i], min_val = arr[i]; for k from i to j: update max_val and min_val; total += (max_val - min_val); return total.

examples.trace:
Array: [1,2,3], n=3
Subarrays:
1. [1]: max=1, min=1, diff=0, total=0
2. [1,2]: max=2, min=1, diff=1, total=1
3. [1,2,3]: max=3, min=1, diff=2, total=3
4. [2]: max=2, min=2, diff=0, total=3
5. [2,3]: max=3, min=2, diff=1, total=4
6. [3]: max=3, min=3, diff=0, total=4
Final total = 4

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Max and Min for a Given Subarray
ladders.desc: Given start and end indices, find maximum and minimum values in that subarray
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: to compute the range (max-min) of any subarray, we need to find both its maximum and minimum elements. This works by scanning through the specified portion of the array while maintaining running maximum and minimum values. We initialize max_val and min_val with the first element of the subarray, then iterate through the remaining elements, updating max_val whenever we find a larger value and min_val whenever we find a smaller value. The algorithm: function find_max_min(arr, start, end): max_val = arr[start]; min_val = arr[start]; for i from start+1 to end: if arr[i] > max_val: max_val = arr[i]; if arr[i] < min_val: min_val = arr[i]; return (max_val, min_val). Time complexity is O(k) where k = end-start+1 (the subarray length). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds directly into the full problem - once we can compute max and min for any subarray, we just need to apply this to all possible subarrays. Common mistakes: 1) Initializing with wrong values (should use first element, not 0 or infinity), 2) Using <= or >= instead of strict comparisons (depends on problem definition), 3) Not handling empty subarrays (shouldn't occur in this context). The real pattern is straightforward linear scan with two tracking variables.

examples.trace:
Subarray: [3,1,4]
Initialize: max=3, min=3
Index 1: arr[1]=1, 1<3 so min=1, max stays 3
Index 2: arr[2]=4, 4>3 so max=4, 4>1 so min stays 1
Final: max=4, min=1

Subarray: [-2,0,8,3]
Initialize: max=-2, min=-2
Index 2: arr[2]=0, 0>-2 so max=0, 0>-2 so min stays -2
Index 3: arr[3]=8, 8>0 so max=8, 8>-2 so min stays -2
Index 4: arr[4]=3, 3<8 so max stays 8, 3>-2 so min stays -2
Final: max=8, min=-2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Generate all possible start and end indices for contiguous subarrays
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic of the brute force approach: generating all possible contiguous subarrays of an array. The intuition is that to examine every subarray, we need to consider every possible starting position and every possible ending position that comes after it. This works because any contiguous subarray is uniquely defined by its start index i and end index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-1 (all possible starts), and the inner loop iterates j from i to n-1 (all possible ends for that start). For each (i,j) pair, we have a subarray arr[i..j]. The total number of subarrays is n(n+1)/2, which grows quadratically with n. Time complexity for just generating these indices is O(n²). Space complexity is O(1) as we only store loop indices. This connects to the full problem by providing the framework - once we can iterate through all subarrays, we just need to compute max-min for each. Common mistakes: 1) Starting j from 0 instead of i (would create non-contiguous or duplicate subarrays), 2) Using j < i condition (would miss subarrays), 3) Not understanding that subarrays must be contiguous. The real pattern is: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): process subarray arr[i..j].

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Given two numbers, compute their absolute difference (max-min)
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to the problem: computing the range of a subarray, which is defined as the difference between its maximum and minimum values. The intuition is simple - the range measures the spread of values in the subarray. For any two numbers a and b where a is the maximum and b is the minimum, the range is a - b. This always gives a non-negative result since a ≥ b. The algorithm is trivial: range = max_val - min_val. Time and space complexity are both O(1). This connects to the sub-routine level - once we have max and min for a subarray, we compute their difference. Common mistakes: 1) Computing min-max instead of max-min (would give negative values), 2) Using absolute value unnecessarily (max-min is already non-negative), 3) Not ensuring max and min are correctly identified before subtraction. The real pattern is straightforward subtraction after verifying which is larger.

examples.trace:
max = 5, min = 2
range = max - min = 5 - 2 = 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Larger of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and return the larger one
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly when finding the maximum value in a subarray. The intuition is basic comparison: given two numbers, we need to determine which is larger (or if they're equal, either can be considered larger depending on implementation). This works by using a simple conditional check. The algorithm: function larger(a, b): if a > b: return a else: return b. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to the building block level - when scanning through a subarray to find the maximum, we repeatedly apply this operation to compare the current maximum with the next element. Common mistakes: 1) Using >= instead of > (affects behavior with equal values), 2) Not handling edge cases like negative numbers or zeros (comparison works the same), 3) Confusing with finding the smaller number. The real pattern is a simple if-else or using the max() function if available.

examples.trace:
Compare 3 and 7
3 > 7? No
So larger is 7

Compare 5 and 5
5 > 5? No (they're equal)
So return 5 (could return either)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to access elements in an array using indices. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has an index (position) starting from 0. To access an element, we use the array name followed by the index in brackets. This works because the array's starting address is known, and each element occupies a fixed amount of memory, so the address of the ith element can be calculated as base_address + i * element_size. The algorithm is simply: element = arr[index]. Time and space complexity are O(1) for access. This connects to the basic operation level - to compare two numbers in an array, we first need to access them. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (many languages use 0-based), 2) Accessing out of bounds (index < 0 or ≥ array length), 3) Confusing the index with the value stored at that index. The real pattern is straightforward: declare array, then use arr[i] to get the element at position i.

examples.trace:
Array has 3 elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30
Accessing arr[1] gives the element at position 1, which is 20

Array: [5,2,8,1]
Indices: 0→5, 1→2, 2→8, 3→1
arr[3] accesses element at index 3, which is 1

approaches.name: Optimized Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all subarrays by nested loops, but maintain running max and min as the subarray expands to avoid recomputation. This reduces time complexity compared to the brute force approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Subarray Ranges with Optimized Brute Force
ladders.desc: Compute sum of (max-min) for all subarrays using nested loops with running max/min tracking.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires calculating the sum of (maximum - minimum) for every possible contiguous subarray of an array. The naive brute force approach would be O(n³) since for each subarray we'd scan to find max and min. The optimized brute force approach reduces this to O(n²) by using nested loops where the outer loop fixes the starting index, and the inner loop expands the subarray from that start. As we expand, we maintain running maximum and minimum values, updating them when we encounter new elements. This avoids rescanning the entire subarray for each new endpoint. The intuition is that when extending a subarray by one element to the right, the new max is simply max(previous_max, new_element) and similarly for min. This incremental update is the key optimization. The algorithm: Initialize total_sum = 0. For each start index i from 0 to n-1: set current_max = current_min = arr[i]. For each end index j from i to n-1: update current_max = max(current_max, arr[j]), current_min = min(current_min, arr[j]), add (current_max - current_min) to total_sum. Return total_sum. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have n starting points and at most n ending points per start. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is straightforward but still quadratic, making it suitable for moderate n (up to ~10³). Common mistakes include incorrectly initializing current_max/min inside loops or forgetting to update them. This feeds directly into the problem by implementing the complete solution.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 2, 3], n=3. Initialize total_sum=0. Start i=0: current_max=1, current_min=1. j=0: subarray [1], max=1, min=1, diff=0, total_sum=0. j=1: subarray [1,2], update current_max=max(1,2)=2, current_min=min(1,2)=1, diff=1, total_sum=1. j=2: subarray [1,2,3], update current_max=max(2,3)=3, current_min=min(1,3)=1, diff=2, total_sum=3. Start i=1: current_max=2, current_min=2. j=1: subarray [2], diff=0, total_sum=3. j=2: subarray [2,3], update current_max=max(2,3)=3, current_min=min(2,3)=2, diff=1, total_sum=4. Start i=2: current_max=3, current_min=3. j=2: subarray [3], diff=0, total_sum=4. Return 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Range Sum for All Subarrays Starting at a Fixed Index
ladders.desc: Given a start index, compute sum of (max-min) for all subarrays extending from that start to each possible end.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the optimized brute force approach. For a fixed starting index i in an array, we need to calculate the contribution of all subarrays that begin at i. The intuition is to expand the subarray incrementally to the right, maintaining the current maximum and minimum as we go. Instead of recomputing max and min from scratch for each new endpoint j (which would be O(n) per subarray, leading to O(n³) total), we update them in O(1) time by comparing with the new element. This reduces the per-start computation from O(n²) to O(n). The algorithm: Initialize current_max = current_min = arr[i]. Initialize sum_for_start = 0. For j from i to n-1: update current_max = max(current_max, arr[j]), current_min = min(current_min, arr[j]), add (current_max - current_min) to sum_for_start. Return sum_for_start. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) since we loop through at most n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each start index i, and the total sum is the sum of all sum_for_start values. Common mistakes include not resetting current_max/min for each new start, or incorrectly handling empty subarrays (but here j starts at i, so subarray has at least one element). This sub-routine encapsulates the optimization that makes the overall approach O(n²).

examples.trace: Start i=0, arr[0]=3. current_max=3, current_min=3, sum=0. j=0: subarray [3], max=3, min=3, diff=0, sum=0. j=1: subarray [3,1], update max=max(3,1)=3, min=min(3,1)=1, diff=2, sum=2. j=2: subarray [3,1,4], update max=max(3,4)=4, min=min(1,4)=1, diff=3, sum=5. Wait, correction: For j=2, after update: max=4, min=1, diff=3, sum becomes 2+3=5. Actually, let's recalc: Start sum=0. j=0: diff=0, sum=0. j=1: diff=3-1=2, sum=2. j=2: diff=4-1=3, sum=5. Output should be 5. My earlier output 7 was incorrect. Correct output is 5.

Start i=1, arr[1]=5. current_max=5, current_min=5, sum=0. j=1: [5], diff=0, sum=0. j=2: [5,3], update max=max(5,3)=5, min=min(5,3)=3, diff=2, sum=2. j=3: [5,3,1], update max=max(5,1)=5, min=min(3,1)=1, diff=4, sum=6. Wait, that gives sum=6, but expected 10? Let's check: For subarrays starting at index 1: [5] diff=0, [5,3] diff=2, [5,3,1] diff=4, total=6. But maybe I misinterpreted. Actually, the problem sums max-min for all subarrays, so for start=1, we have these three subarrays, sum=0+2+4=6. If expected is 10, perhaps I need to include more? No, this sub-routine only does from start to end. So output should be 6. I'll correct: output is 6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Running Maximum and Minimum During Subarray Expansion
ladders.desc: Update max and min incrementally as a subarray grows by one element to the right.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that optimizes the brute force approach. When expanding a subarray by including a new element at the end, instead of scanning the entire subarray to find new max and min (which would be O(k) for a subarray of length k), we can update in O(1) by comparing the new element with the current max and min. The intuition is that the maximum of a longer subarray is simply the maximum of the previous maximum and the new element, and similarly for the minimum. This works because max and min are associative and commutative over the set of elements: max(a,b,c) = max(max(a,b), c). So, if we know max of subarray [i..j-1], then max of [i..j] is max(previous_max, arr[j]). This incremental update is key to reducing time complexity. The algorithm for expanding: Given current_max and current_min for subarray [i..j-1], to include arr[j]: set current_max = max(current_max, arr[j]), current_min = min(current_min, arr[j]). Then compute diff = current_max - current_min. This is done in constant time. Time complexity per expansion is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the sub-routine by being the inner step in the loop over j. Common mistakes include forgetting to update both max and min, or incorrectly initializing them when starting a new subarray. This logic is fundamental to many sliding window and dynamic programming problems where properties are maintained incrementally.

examples.input: Current subarray: [3, 1] with max=3, min=1. Add element 4.
examples.trace: Start with current_max=3, current_min=1 for subarray [3,1]. New element is 4. Update: current_max = max(3, 4) = 4. current_min = min(1, 4) = 1. Diff = 4 - 1 = 3. So new subarray [3,1,4] has max=4, min=1.

examples.input: Current subarray: [5, 2, 7] with max=7, min=2. Add element 1.
examples.trace: Start with current_max=7, current_min=2 for subarray [5,2,7]. New element is 1. Update: current_max = max(7, 1) = 7. current_min = min(2, 1) = 1. Diff = 7 - 1 = 6. So new subarray [5,2,7,1] has max=7, min=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays of an array using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is about enumerating every contiguous subarray of an array, which is necessary before we can compute their properties like max-min. A contiguous subarray is defined by a start index i and an end index j where i ≤ j, and it includes all elements from i to j inclusive. The total number of subarrays for an array of length n is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). The intuition is to systematically cover all pairs (i,j). The algorithm uses two nested loops: outer loop for i from 0 to n-1, inner loop for j from i to n-1. For each pair, the subarray is arr[i..j]. This generates all subarrays without duplication. Time complexity is O(n²) for the iteration itself. Space complexity is O(1) if we just iterate indices. This feeds into the core logic by providing the framework where we can apply the running max/min updates as j increases for a fixed i. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., j starting from 0 instead of i), or misunderstanding contiguity (subarrays must be consecutive indices). This building block is fundamental in array processing problems.

examples.trace: n=2. i=0: j=0 -> subarray [1]. j=1 -> subarray [1,2]. i=1: j=1 -> subarray [2]. Total 3 subarrays.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Maximum and Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Find the larger (max) and smaller (min) of two given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used repeatedly in the algorithm. Given two numbers a and b, we need to determine which is larger and which is smaller. The intuition is straightforward comparison. For max: if a > b, then max = a, else max = b. For min: if a < b, then min = a, else min = b. This can be done using conditional statements or built-in functions like max() and min() in most programming languages. Time complexity is O(1), space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block as it's used to update running max and min when expanding subarrays. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators (e.g., >= instead of > for max, but typically it doesn't matter if equal) or not handling negative numbers correctly (comparison works the same). This operation is essential in many algorithms, from sorting to optimization.

examples.trace: Compare 3 and 7. Since 3 < 7, max is 7, min is 3.
Compare -2 and -5. Since -2 > -5, max is -2, min is -5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and iterate with loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the problem. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (usually starting from 0). For example, in array arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that indices allow direct access to any element in constant time. To process all elements, we use loops: for i in range(0, n): access arr[i]. This is fundamental because in the problem, we need to traverse the array to consider all subarrays. Time complexity for iterating an array of length n is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if we just access elements. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the elements to compare. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing arr[n] which is out of bounds) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. This concept is essential for any array-based algorithm.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 1 refers to the second element, which is 8.

examples.input: Array: [1, 2, 3, 4], iterate and print each element
examples.trace: Use loop i from 0 to 3: i=0 print 1, i=1 print 2, i=2 print 3, i=3 print 4.
